fields.definition_origin: Daniel Kahneman, Barbara Fredrickson, Charles Schreiber, Donald Redelmeier (1993) — 'When more pain is preferred to less: Adding a better end' (Psychological Science 4(6):401-405). Extended by Kahneman & Redelmeier (1996) on colonoscopy patients (NEJM): memory of duration is replaced by memory of peak intensity + ending. Codified in Kahneman's 'Thinking, Fast and Slow' (2011) as the experiencing-self vs remembering-self distinction. Applied to UX by Nielsen Norman Group (Lexie Kane, December 2018, 'The Peak-End Rule: How Impressions Become Memories'). Onboarding-specific application by Cal AI's mid-onboarding peak placement, Mau Baron's narrative arc, and PMC11343653 (2024) mental-health peak-end review.
fields.core_mechanism: Human memory does not record duration accurately ('duration neglect') — instead it summarizes an experience as a weighted average of (a) the emotional peak (best or worst moment) and (b) the ending state. The remembering-self, not the experiencing-self, drives whether a user returns. For onboarding, the user's first session IS the experience that the remembering-self will retrieve when deciding whether to open the app on Day 1. Therefore: onboarding total minutes matter less for retention than (a) at least one engineered above-baseline peak (the AI plan reveal at step N-1), and (b) a positive close state (the post-paywall empty state must NOT degrade — a celebration / personalized welcome / first-day plan). A flat 'pretty good' onboarding loses to one with a real spike and a satisfying close. Mau Baron's quote 'as long as it's adding value and telling a story, the longer the better' is peak-end logic explicit: long flows that contain the peak win.
fields.neuroscience_correlate: Memory consolidation is biased toward emotionally salient events via amygdala-hippocampus interaction (LaBar & Cabeza 2006, Nature Reviews Neuroscience). The remembering-self/experiencing-self distinction maps loosely onto default-mode-network (autobiographical recall) vs moment-to-moment limbic/sensory processing. Dopaminergic prediction error at the ending may explain why a positive close is over-weighted in retrospective evaluation (Schultz 1997 reward prediction error). Direct fMRI evidence for the peak-end rule itself is limited; the effect is established behaviorally.
fields.empirical_evidence: (1) Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber & Redelmeier (1993) — cold-pressor study, n=32, participants preferred a longer painful trial that ended with milder pain over a shorter trial that ended at peak pain. Effect was robust to debriefing. (2) Redelmeier & Kahneman (1996) — colonoscopy patients (n=154 + n=109); patients in elongated 'softer ending' condition rated procedure as less unpleasant and more likely to return for follow-up. (3) Frontiers in Psychology 2019 (Müller, Sirianni, Addessi) — confirmed peak-end robustness across complex heterogeneous experiences. (4) PMC11343653 (2024) — peak-end review in mental-health contexts, confirms application to depression rumination, therapy memory, digital-health adherence. (5) UX industry validation: NN/g (2018, Lexie Kane), Laws of UX both treat peak-end as foundational. Duolingo, Headspace, TurboTax cited as canonical applications. (6) Onboarding-specific case: Cal AI deploys mid-onboarding App-Store-review prompt RIGHT AFTER the AI plan reveal (the engineered peak) — review rate is materially higher than typical end-of-app-prompts. (7) Mau Baron Prayer Lock — narrative arc designed around a peak (AI prayer reveal) and a soft positive close (first-prayer-saved-to-home), 5min→15min flow tripled organic conversion. (8) Counter-evidence: Headway's overly-celebratory ending without a sustained Day-2 hook produces high D1 but lower D7 retention — peak alone doesn't compound.
fields.measurable_outcome_metric: Day-1, Day-7, Day-30 retention curves (peak-end engineered cohort vs flat cohort), repeat-session rate, NPS at session-end, 5-star App Store review rate, D1 push open rate (peak primes the remembering-self for the Day-1 push to feel welcome), share / referral rate immediately post-onboarding, willingness-to-repeat-task (Kahneman's original DV adapted as 'would you go through this again?').
fields.conversion_lift_benchmark: Cal AI mid-onboarding review prompt placed at peak (post-AI-plan-reveal) yields review rates materially above the App Store mid-app-prompt baseline (industry practitioner reports cite 5-10x lift, unverified at scale). Mau Baron Prayer Lock narrative arc with engineered peak: 3x organic conversion lift on 5min→15min flow. NN/g 2018 — Spotify Wrapped is canonical scale-of-the-year peak-end; one of most-shared product moments ever. Adapty 2026: onboarding paywall converts 1.35% (highest of any placement) when paywall is positioned right after engineered peak.
fields.engineering_tactics: (1) Engineered peak placement at step N-1 (immediately before paywall): AI plan reveal with 4-7s theatrical loader animating personalization computation, then full-bleed reveal of named plan card with 3 stat cards revealed in stagger (200ms each), brand-color confetti motif, Reanimated 3 spring scale-in (damping:14, stiffness:160), sensoryFeedback .success on iOS, VibrationEffect.EFFECT_HEAVY_CLICK on Android. (2) Engineered close (post-paywall, even if user declines): never end on a friction screen; show personalized 'Day 1 of 30' welcome card with the user's name/plan visible. (3) Mid-onboarding review prompt — Cal AI pattern: deploy App Store native review request RIGHT after AI plan reveal, before paywall (peak emotional state, highest grant rate). (4) Forbid upsell modals at the close — converting the peak into 'Try Premium!' kills the positive remembering-self memory. (5) Notifications themselves are mini-sessions — engineer their open-state to feel like a peak (visible plan name, today's recommended action) not a guilt trip. (6) Day-1 push must reinforce the onboarding peak ('Your Cal Plan starts today') — this is what compounds the peak into D7 retention. (7) Avoid mid-flow valleys: instrument session sentiment per step, identify negative peaks (errors, confusion), fix those first.
fields.real_world_examples: (1) Cal AI step ~30 — AI macro plan reveal with 4-7s loader → full-bleed plan card → mid-onboarding App Store review prompt → side-by-side plan paywall. The peak (reveal) and the engineered review prompt are placed at maximum emotional salience. (2) Duolingo end-of-onboarding — first lesson completion celebration (Duo mascot Rive animation, XP burst, streak start, big 'See you tomorrow' close). (3) Mau Baron Prayer Lock — AI-personalized prayer reveal as the peak; first-prayer-saved-to-home as the close. (4) Headspace onboarding ending — slow fade to a calming end-card with the day's insight + tomorrow's session preview (peak + soft close). (5) Spotify Wrapped — annual peak-end at scale; one of the most-shared product moments ever. (6) Apple Watch onboarding — close-the-rings introduction with rings-collapse celebration animation; the haptic + visual at the close is the peak. (7) Phantom Wallet successful first-send — checkmark animation + transaction-share link as the peak ending. (8) Anti-example: a long onboarding that ends with 'Sign up to continue' modal — the close IS the friction; remembering-self encodes the entire flow as adversarial.
fields.mobile_vs_web_diffs: Mobile has stronger peak-end leverage because of haptic + audio + screen-light at the close. Use sensoryFeedback (.success) on iOS 17+, VibrationEffect.createPredefined(EFFECT_HEAVY_CLICK) on Android. Ensure close animation runs at 60+ fps. Mobile sessions are also shorter and more frequent — peak-end engineering matters per session many times per day. Web peaks rely on visual + audio only; confetti.js or Lottie can substitute, honor prefers-reduced-motion. Mobile-primary; web equivalent: full-bleed celebration screen + share-to-social CTA + email follow-up that re-references the peak.
fields.platform_design_language_alignment: Aligned with both. Apple HIG 'Feedback' chapter explicitly recommends celebrating completion; Liquid Glass (iOS 26+) lends itself to expressive end-states with translucent material + dynamic morphing. Material 3 Expressive (I/O 2025, 18,000-participant research) measured 'meaningful celebration moments' as a win factor — its expressive shape system is the substrate for engineered peaks. Both platforms ship-faster guidance: budget 10-15% of design effort on the peak + close screens.
fields.category_fit: Universal — every category benefits. Critical for: wellness/habit (Cal AI, Headspace, Calm — peak-end IS the engagement mechanic), AI-wrapper (the LLM-generated reveal IS the engineered peak), edtech (Duolingo lesson-end celebration is canonical), gaming (level-complete peaks + soft close to next), social (Snapchat memory, Apple Photos memories are peak-end at scale). Less critical for: pure-utility apps with no session structure (calculator). DO NOT engineer peaks at the expense of authenticity — over-engineered confetti users want to skip after their fifth session.
fields.tooling_options: Reanimated 3 + react-native-skia (custom particle peak animations), Rive (mascot celebrations like Duolingo's Duo), Lottie (cheaper celebration assets if Skia/Rive too heavy), iOS sensoryFeedback API + Android VibrationEffect, Howler.js / react-native-sound (audio sting under 200ms), PostHog session recording (verify peak lands), Mixpanel funnel (D1/D7/D30 retention by peak-engineered cohort), Adapty (paywall right after peak), Apple StoreKit RequestReview API (mid-onboarding review prompt at peak).
fields.ai_dependency: optional: peak-end works without AI — Duolingo's celebration is hand-animated, Apple Watch ring-close is deterministic. AI amplifies the peak when used (Cal AI's LLM-generated personalized plan IS the peak's content). When AI is used: GPT-4-class reasoning, ~1-3s latency, 4-7s theatrical loader masks it. Offline-fallback static peak required (cached pre-generated plan template).
fields.claude_design_application: Instruct Claude to engineer an explicit peak + close per onboarding flow. Example fragment 1: 'For the AI plan reveal screen (step N-1), engineer the peak: full-bleed personalized plan card, custom react-native-skia particle burst (24 particles, brand color, 700ms duration), Reanimated 3 spring scale-in for the achievement card (damping:14, stiffness:160), sensoryFeedback .success on iOS, VibrationEffect.EFFECT_HEAVY_CLICK on Android, audio sting under 200ms (mute-respecting). Then deploy StoreKit RequestReview API mid-flow IF user-was-active-7-days prerequisite met. THEN paywall. Forbid upsell modal stacked on the peak.' Example fragment 2: 'Audit the onboarding flow: identify (a) the peak moment (b) the close moment. Output a flow → peak → close table as a comment block. For any flow missing one, propose a fix. The close MUST be a positive state — never a friction screen, never a generic Success! toast.'
fields.stitch_application: Stitch must be told to design TWO frames (peak + close) since it tends to default to a single end-screen. Example fragment 1: 'Design TWO frames for the onboarding-end flow. Frame 1 (PEAK — AI plan reveal): full-bleed celebration with custom-illustrated motif (NOT generic confetti SVG), the user\'s personalized plan name in 56pt rounded display type (DM Serif Display, NOT Inter), 3 stat cards revealed in stagger (each in different bold accent color), brand color #FF6B47 particle burst centered. Frame 2 (CLOSE — post-paywall first-day card): calm muted card with \'Day 1 of 30\' counter, plan name top-right, primary CTA \'Start your first action\', 24pt body type, no upsell, dark mode #0E1116 background.' Example fragment 2: 'Mid-onboarding review prompt placed between peak and paywall: Apple native review modal pattern with App Store star + review CTA. Forbid showing review prompt before peak or after paywall.'
fields.ai_codegen_failure_mode: AI codegen produces a generic 'Success!' toast at the end, treats end-screen as plumbing, no peak engineering, no haptic/audio integration, and stacks an upsell modal on the close. Counter-prompt: 'The peak + close are the most retention-leveraged screens of the entire app — over-budget polish here. Forbid generic Success! toast. Require: custom celebration animation (Reanimated/Skia/Rive — NOT a stock Lottie), haptic + audio + visual triple-channel feedback, an explicit close state separate from the peak. Forbid upsell modal on the success/peak screen. The close must show a positive state (Day 1 of 30 plan card, named plan, next action). Output a comment block listing the peak move and the close move before writing JSX.'
fields.anti_patterns: (1) Upsell modal at the close — converts the peak into adversarial close; DFA dark-pattern radar. (2) Flat session — no peak engineered, fade-out close. Predicts higher D1 churn. (3) Ending on friction — error toast, sync conflict, 'Are you sure?' modal as the last screen. (4) Front-loaded peaks only — celebration on screen 1, then nothing for 30 days; users adapt and the peak becomes baseline (NN/g warning). (5) Manipulative streak-rescue at close — using peak-end to instill guilt ('Don't lose your streak') is regulatory hot zone. (6) Over-designed peaks — 4-second confetti users want to skip after fifth session. (7) Wasted Loading antipattern paired with peak — a blank loader before the peak is the *opposite* of theatrical (which is engineered loading with narrative). (8) Generic Celebration — peak design that doesn't match brand archetype (Lover archetype getting a Hero confetti burst).
fields.regulatory_risk: Medium. Peak-end engineering itself is neutral, but two specific applications carry regulatory exposure: (1) streak-save / loss-aversion notifications targeting under-18 users — UK AADC, California AADC, EU Digital Fairness Act (Q4 2026 draft) all flag streak-induced compulsion; BEUC SHEIN complaint (June 2025) cited dark-pattern engagement loops including loss-framed peaks. (2) Variable-reward peaks (slot-machine style randomized celebrations) draw scrutiny under DSA dark-pattern rules. Mitigate by giving users explicit control (streak freeze, opt-out of celebration animations) and avoiding loss-framed messaging. Mid-onboarding review prompt at peak: ensure compliance with App Store Review Guideline 1.1.5 (no incentivized reviews) and StoreKit RequestReview rate-limiting.
fields.platform_constraints_2026: Apple StoreKit RequestReview API rate-limits review prompts to 3 per 365 days — use the mid-onboarding peak deployment carefully. Apple Guideline 1.1.5 — no incentivized review requests. Apple Guideline 3.1.2 effective 2026-01 — toggle paywalls banned; the post-peak paywall must use side-by-side plan selection. Material 3 Expressive recognition-speed metrics inform Play Store quality scoring. WCAG 2.2 §2.3.3 Animation — peak animations must respect prefers-reduced-motion.
fields.accessibility_implications: Peak animations must respect prefers-reduced-motion (replace particle burst with crossfade ≤200ms). Sound-on celebrations must respect mute switch. VoiceOver/TalkBack must announce peak state ('Plan generated. Day 1 of 30 ready.'). Dynamic-type to 200% must not break the peak card layout. WCAG 2.2 §2.3.1 Three Flashes — celebration animations must not exceed flash thresholds. Touch target on close-state CTA ≥44×44pt iOS / 48dp Android, also ≥24×24px (WCAG 2.2 §2.5.8). Streak-loss peaks can create anxiety for users with OCD, ADHD, depression — provide explicit 'pause streak' affordance.
fields.pitfalls_caveats: (1) Adaptation — a celebration that fires every session becomes baseline within a week; rotate or scale up at milestones (Day 7, Day 30, Day 100). (2) Cultural calibration — celebration register varies (loud/expressive in US, quieter in JP/KR; full-screen confetti can feel embarrassing on a Tokyo subway). (3) Performance — heavy particle effects on low-end Android can drop frames during the most important moment. (4) Over-engineered peak overshadows the actual product value (NN/g caution). (5) The peak must MATCH the user's archetype + intent — generic celebration feels off. (6) Mid-onboarding review prompt risk: if peak doesn't actually land (LLM fails), the review request fires at a flat moment and burns the StoreKit rate-limit slot. (7) Day-2 onward must compound the peak — if Day 2 has no peak, D7 retention drops despite great D1.
fields.cross_cultural_validity: Peak-end rule itself is biologically grounded (memory bias) and replicates broadly. Cultural calibration applies to expression intensity: US/EU markets tolerate exuberant celebration; JP, KR, DE prefer restrained celebration (subtle haptic + small chime, not full-screen confetti). Microcopy register must localize — 'Crushing it!' translates poorly to most non-English markets. Streak-save and loss-aversion peaks should be softened in cultures with stronger collectivist norms where social shame is already heightened. RTL: celebration animations must mirror direction of progress (XP bar fills right-to-left in Arabic).
fields.prescribed_action: Engineer one explicit peak at step N-1 (AI plan reveal with theatrical loader + named-plan reveal + custom celebration) and one positive close state (post-paywall Day-1 plan card, never an upsell modal). Optionally deploy a mid-onboarding StoreKit RequestReview RIGHT after the peak. Forbid ending the onboarding flow on a friction screen, generic Success toast, or upsell modal stacked on the celebration.
fields.questionnaire_relevance: The questionnaire's last 2 steps must engineer the peak: step N-1 is the AI plan reveal (peak), step N is the close state (paywall + Day-1 card). The questionnaire's middle should NOT contain a higher peak than step N-1 — otherwise duration neglect makes users encode the entire flow as 'the questionnaire was the best part, the paywall ruined it'.
fields.aha_relevance: The engineered peak IS the Aha moment for long-onboarding apps. The peak's success is what makes Day-1 retention happen — the user wakes up the next day remembering the peak (remembering-self) and the app's home-screen icon triggers anticipation rather than guilt. Peak-end and Aha are the same engineering target on long flows.
fields.emotional_archetype_alignment: Universal — every archetype needs a peak + close. Peak expression varies: Magician (transformation reveal), Hero (mastery achievement), Caregiver (gentle warmth, less exuberant), Jester (playful surprise), Sage (revelation of insight), Outlaw (rebellious 'we did it'). Pairs with .agent/research/emotional-design/results/Peak_End_Rule_Onboarding.json (deeper foundational treatment) and .agent/research/emotional-design/results/Jungian_Brand_Archetypes.json. Conflicts: a Caregiver app firing a Hero-confetti peak feels archetype-mismatched.
fields.anti_pattern_paired: Wasted Loading Screens (the loader IS the peak setup, never blank); Generic Celebration (mismatched archetype voice kills the peak); Upsell-At-Close (converts peak to adversarial close, DFA risk); Premature Authentication (peak can't form if user is gated before value).
sources: [The Peak-End Rule: How Impressions Become Memories — Nielsen Norman Group (Lexie Kane, 2018)](https://www.nngroup.com/articles/peak-end-rule/)
[Kahneman, Fredrickson, Schreiber, Redelmeier (1993) — Adding a Better End](https://kenthendricks.com/peak-end-rule/)
[Redelmeier & Kahneman (1996) — Patients' memories of painful medical treatments](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8657437/)
[Frontiers in Psychology — Robustness of Peak-End Rule (2019)](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2019.01705/full)
[A Review of the Peak-End Rule in Mental Health Contexts — PMC (2024)](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11343653/)
